title: Discipleship, Part 4: Cross-Generational Discipleship
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 4 - Cross-Generational Discipleship - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. Every Segment of the Church Is Called to Learn and Change
subpoints: The old adage 'an old dog can't learn new tricks' is unbiblical -- to be a Christian is to be ever changing
Sanctification means we are always being transformed; stagnation is evidence of not being in Christ
We are not just hearers of the Word but doers (Matthew 7; James 1)
Paul tells Titus to speak things proper for sound doctrine -- a mix of theology and practical application

outline.title: 2. The Older Men (Titus 2:2)
subpoints: To be sober (not drunk, level-headed and stable), reverent (serious and worthy of respect), temperate (self-controlled and disciplined)
Sound in faith (healthy belief AND practice), sound in love (older men are to model love for the church), sound in patience (endurance to the finish)
The 'older man' is a relative term depending on context, not a fixed age category
The older men train the younger men by their example and instruction

outline.title: 3. The Older Women (Titus 2:3-4)
subpoints: Reverent in behavior -- conduct appropriate for a holy person dedicated to God's service
Not slanderers -- 'devil' (diabolos) means slanderer; gossip is at a deeper level of heinousness than many sins we more commonly condemn
Not given to much wine
Teachers of good things -- to admonish (impart wisdom to) the younger women

outline.title: 4. Seven Things the Older Women Teach the Younger Women (Titus 2:4-5)
subpoints: To love their husbands and children -- the need for exhortation implies there are times you don't feel like it; but you can learn
To be discreet (self-controlled, right thinking) and chaste (sexually and emotionally pure)
To be homemakers (managers of the home) -- not a prohibition on working outside the home, but a mindset of primary focus
To be obedient to their own husbands -- submission applies to the wife's own husband, not to men in general
The goal: that the word of God may not be blasphemed; Peter adds the gospel goes forth when wives live this way

outline.title: 5. The Younger Men (Titus 2:6-8)
subpoints: To be sober-minded -- thinking things through from the perspective of God's word
Titus himself is to be a pattern of good works, integrity in doctrine, reverence, and incorruptibility
The younger man is to be trained by the older man in all these things
These dynamics require intentional relationships -- you cannot speak to someone you've never talked with

summary:
This fourth installment of the Discipleship series focuses on the cross-generational nature of discipleship in the local church, drawn from Titus 2:1-8. Pastor Tito Lyro establishes the foundational premise that every segment of the church -- old and young alike -- is called to learn, grow, and change. Claiming 'you can't teach an old dog new tricks' as a reason for not changing is sinful and unbiblical; to be a Christian is to be ever-changing through sanctification.

The lesson then works through each group Paul addresses. The older men are called to be sober (level-headed), reverent (worthy of respect), temperate (self-controlled), and sound in faith, love, and patience. The word 'sound' means healthy -- a healthy faith includes both what you believe and how you live it out. The older men carry the responsibility of modeling love to the entire congregation.

The older women are called to reverent behavior, freedom from slander (the word for 'devil' in Greek means 'slanderer'), sobriety, and to be teachers of good things. Their primary teaching role is directed toward the younger women, and there are seven things they are to impart: love for husband and children, discretion, chastity, homemaking, and submission to their own husbands. The section on homemaking is carefully nuanced -- it is not a prohibition on working outside the home (Proverbs 31's capable woman is quite active economically) but a matter of primary focus and mindset.

The younger men are called to be sober-minded -- to think through life from the perspective of God's word. Titus himself is to be a pattern of good works, doctrinal integrity, reverence, and incorruptibility. The lesson closes by emphasizing that these cross-generational discipleship dynamics require actual relationships -- you cannot speak life into someone you've never had a real conversation with.
transcript:
## Discipleship, Part 4: Cross-Generational Discipleship

*Pastor Tito Lyro -- Sunday School*

---

The rest of us, if you have a Bible, turn to Titus chapter 2. We're continuing our series on discipleship. Today we want to consider cross-generational discipleship -- as the title says, it should be pretty obvious: discipleship that goes across ages. So older people, younger people, all involved in discipling one another.

The Bible has something to say about every single relationship we encounter in life -- husband, wife, parent, child, employer, employee, friends, older people, younger people. The Bible talks about all of them. Paul speaks of the dynamics of relationship as they pertain to learning and teaching in Titus chapter 2, verses 1 through 8.

### Every Segment Is Called to Learn and Change

I want you to notice as we go through this lesson that **every segment in the church is called to learn and grow and change and be transformed, regardless of age.** The old adage, "an old dog can't learn new tricks," is not biblical. If you ever claim that as the reason you're not changing, you're claiming a sinful, unbiblical approach to life. To be a Christian is to be ever-changing. That is the doctrine of sanctification. If we're not changing, then we're not really in Christ.

Remember in Matthew 7, Jesus finishes the Sermon on the Mount and says: if you hear these words and don't do them, you're like a foolish builder who builds on sand. The wise builder is the one who hears the word of God and *does* them. James says the same thing in James 1-2. We are not just hearers of the Word but doers.

Now, why is Titus supposed to teach these things? Three reasons:

1. There were false teachers in Crete (Titus 1:10-16), and the congregation needed to be equipped to recognize and refute them.
2. Paul tells Titus to do this because this is the *normal* Christian life -- not super-Christian goals to attain, but what it looks like to live as a Christian every day.
3. The pastor's job is to equip the saints for the work of ministry (Ephesians 4:11) so they can disciple one another.

Notice also that Paul does not tell Titus to address just one group. He speaks to older men, older women, younger women, and younger men -- *every* group must learn and grow.

### The Older Men (Titus 2:2)

> *...that the older men be sober, reverent, temperate, sound in faith, in love, in patience.*

The "older man" is a relative term depending on your context. In Greek literature it often referred to men 50 or older, but it's really a sliding scale.

**Sober** -- level-headed, stable. Includes (but is not limited to) not being drunk.

**Reverent** -- serious as opposed to silly. Worthy of respect because of sound judgment. Not gloomy or sour, but possessing gravitas.

**Temperate** -- self-controlled in all things. Structure and discipline must be part of his life.

**Sound in faith** -- healthy in what he believes and how he lives it out. A healthy faith includes doctrine AND practice. Leave either out and the faith is not healthy.

**Sound in love** -- godly men love and let others know they love them. Notice: Paul places the responsibility for *modeling love* on the older men, not the older women. This is the older men's job in the church.

**Sound in patience** -- the ability to endure to the finish. As the older man approaches the time when he will join the cloud of witnesses, he must show that he is still running his race with perseverance.

The older men will then train, disciple, the younger men in all these things -- so the younger men will grow into being older men who will in turn train the younger men after them.

### The Older Women (Titus 2:3-4)

> *...the older women likewise, that they be reverent in behavior, not slanderers, not given to much wine, teachers of good things -- that they admonish the young women...*

**Reverent in behavior** -- to conduct themselves as is appropriate of a holy person. You should be able to tell this woman is dedicated to the service of the Lord by the way she carries herself day by day in observable ways.

**Not slanderers** -- nothing characterizes a devilish person more than slander and gossip. In fact, the Greek word translated "devil" is *diabolos*, the word for *slanderer*. In Romans 1:18-32, where Paul describes a downward spiral of sin, slander appears at a deeper level of heinousness than homosexuality. We are often up in arms about homosexuality; we need to give the same weight to gossip and slander.

Do you know the difference between gossip and slander? Slander is always false; gossip may be true -- it's just not yours to be talking about. When it starts, it tends to burn through a congregation like wildfire. As you disciple others, ask yourself: *Why am I talking about this person? Are we part of the solution? How is what I'm saying going to make them more conformed to the image of Christ?*

**Not given to much wine.**

**Teachers of good things** -- Titus is to tell the older women to be teachers of good things to the younger women. This is *their* job, not the pastor's. The pastor comes to the older women and instructs them, and they take upon themselves the instruction of the younger women. This makes a lot of sense.

### Seven Things the Older Women Are to Teach the Younger Women (Titus 2:4-5)

> *...that they admonish the young women to love their husbands, to love their children, to be discreet, chaste, homemakers, good, obedient to their own husbands, that the word of God may not be blasphemed.*

The word "admonish" (the NKJV rendering) means to impart wisdom. The older women must impart wisdom to the younger women by word and deed together.

**Seven good, beautiful, and wise things:**

1. **Love their husbands** -- the exhortation implies there are times when you don't feel like loving your husband. But you can *learn* to do so. It's not just a feeling beyond your control. When you don't feel like it: repent, confess it to God, and start loving -- because God gives you the grace.

2. **Love their children** -- same principle. Times when it's hard; you can learn.

3. **Discreet** -- self-controlled, related to wisdom and right thinking.

4. **Chaste** -- a form of the word "holy" related to sexual purity, both physically and emotionally.

5. **Homemakers** -- the idea is *manager of the home*. This is not a prohibition on working outside the home. Look at Proverbs 31:10-31 -- that woman is buying a field, trading in the market, starting small businesses. But her focus is the home. Paul is not saying location, he's saying *mindset*. The younger woman's primary focus is the home. Someone can stay home all day and not fulfill this. Someone can work outside the home and still excel in this. It's a mindset, not a location.

6. **Good** -- the broader word encompassing all these qualities.

7. **Obedient to their own husbands** -- note: *their own* husbands, not men in general. Submission is not to every man but specifically to her own husband. And Paul says this is good, beautiful, and wise.

The goal: *"that the word of God may not be blasphemed."* Peter adds that the gospel goes forth and becomes more beautiful when wives live this way.

Our youth will be a reflection of our older men and older women. That's the dynamics Paul is describing. If the older men and women are doing their job, the younger men and women will grow into who they are supposed to be in Christ.

### The Younger Men (Titus 2:6-8)

> *Likewise, exhort the young men to be sober-minded, in all things showing yourself to be a pattern of good works; in doctrine showing integrity, reverence, incorruptibility, sound speech that cannot be condemned, that one who is an opponent may be ashamed, having nothing evil to say of you.*

The younger men are exhorted to be **sober-minded** -- to think things through from the perspective of God's word.

Titus himself is to be the *pattern* of good works -- his life is the visual aid for the younger men. He is to have integrity in doctrine (knows what he believes), reverence (respects good doctrine by doing what it says), incorruptibility (not letting himself be corrupted by the unbiblical), and sound speech.

These dynamics require **relationships**. You cannot just walk up to someone you've never had a conversation with and tell them to read their Bible more. You have to have the relationship that allows you to carry out these discipleship dynamics. So invest in each other. Get to know people. Talk with people. Pray for people. That way you can see the needs that are there.

Let's pray.
